1.synthesis:
On the day of his death, Moses the "man of God" blesses each of the twelve tribes of Israel in turn — Reuben, Judah, Levi, Benjamin, Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh), Zebulun and Issachar, Gad, Dan, Naphtali, Asher. The blessing is prefaced with a theophanic poem celebrating the Sinai covenant: "The LORD came from Sinai and dawned from Seir upon us; he shone forth from Mount Paran; he came from the ten thousands of holy ones, with flaming fire at his right hand" (v. 2). The individual tribal blessings range from brief (Reuben: may he live and not die, though his numbers are few; Dan: a lion's cub, leaping from Bashan) to extensive (Joseph: blessed by the LORD are his land, with the choicest gifts of heaven above and the deep that crouches beneath — vv. 13-17).

Matthew Henry notes that Moses both closes and opens with the tribes: the preface (vv. 2-5) and conclusion (vv. 26-29) frame the tribal blessings with praise of the LORD as the great Warrior-King who is Israel's salvation. Calvin observes that the blessing of Levi (vv. 8-11) is the most detailed, granting the priestly tribe the Thummim and Urim, the right to teach Jacob, and the privilege of offering incense and burnt offerings — the entire religious apparatus of Israel placed in Levitical hands.
1.historical_context: The blessings of Deuteronomy 33 are consciously parallel to Jacob's deathbed blessings of Genesis 49, with significant differences reflecting Israel's changed circumstances on the threshold of Canaan. Simeon is omitted entirely (perhaps absorbed into Judah, cf. Josh 19:1-9). Joseph's blessing is the longest and most exuberant, anticipating the fruitfulness of Ephraim and Manasseh's territories in the central highlands. Levi's blessing reflects the tribe's post-golden-calf zeal (Exod 32:26-29), which earned them the priestly calling. Keil and Delitzsch note that many of the blessings contain geographical and historical predictions that were fulfilled in the tribal territories assigned in Joshua's time.
1.literary_structure: The chapter is structured as a literary triptych: theophanic preface (vv. 2-5), individual tribal blessings (vv. 6-25), and doxological conclusion (vv. 26-29). The preface establishes God's sovereign presence as the foundation of the blessings — they flow from his initiative, not tribal merit. The conclusion generalizes from the specific tribal blessings to a universal proclamation of the incomparability of Israel's God. The placement of Levi (vv. 8-11) after Reuben and Judah, and before the northern tribes, reflects Levi's central religious function rather than geographic sequence.
1.theological_application: Gill finds in Moses's role as "man of God" (v. 1) a significant title: "being raised up of God, and eminently qualified by him with gifts for the work he was called unto, and by whom he was inspired to say what is after expressed." Matthew Henry draws the blessing's pastoral application: just as Jacob blessed his sons on his deathbed, and just as Christ blessed his disciples before his ascension (Luke 24:50), so Moses's last act is blessing — the man of God departs dispensing grace, not curses. Calvin finds in Levi's blessing a lesson about how unfaithfulness (Levi's founder was violent; Gen 34) can be transformed by zeal for God into a calling of the highest dignity.
1.christology: The theophanic preface (vv. 2-3) — "he came from the ten thousands of holy ones" — is applied in Jude 14 to the coming of Christ with his holy ones in judgment. Matthew Henry notes that Christ closed his earthly ministry with a blessing of his disciples just as Moses closed his ministry with a blessing of the tribes — "like Moses here, in token of parting as friends." The blessing of Levi — those who "kept your word and guarded your covenant" by putting family loyalty second to God (v. 9) — anticipates Christ's teaching that those who love father or mother more than him are not worthy of him (Matt 10:37), and points forward to the new covenant priesthood where all believers are "a royal priesthood" (1 Pet 2:9).

26.synthesis:
The Song of Moses and the Blessing of Moses both close with doxological summaries of who the LORD is, and the closing verses of the blessing are among the most exalted declarations of God's character in the entire Pentateuch: "There is none like God, O Jeshurun, who rides through the heavens to your help, through the skies in his majesty. The eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms" (vv. 26-27). The God who thundered from Sinai, who guided Israel through the wilderness, who assigned the land — this God is Israel's permanent home and support. "Happy are you, O Israel! Who is like you, a people saved by the LORD, the shield of your help, and the sword of your triumph!" (v. 29).

Gill finds the phrase "everlasting arms" particularly rich: these are the arms of divine omnipotence, which "support and uphold his people under all their trials and difficulties, and in all their conflicts with their spiritual enemies; they carry the lambs in his bosom, and gently lead those with young." Matthew Henry draws the contrast between Israel's enemies who "shall come fawning to you" and Israel's God who "drives out the enemy before you" — the completeness of the divine victory that grounds the final beatitude.
26.historical_context: The title "Jeshurun" (v. 26) appears here for the third and final time in Deuteronomy (also 32:15 and 33:5), where it is a term of endearment for Israel meaning "the upright one" — a name that describes what Israel was called to be, used here in the final blessing rather than ironically as in the Song. The image of God "riding through the heavens" (v. 26) appears again in Psalm 68:33 and Psalm 104:3, reflecting the ancient Near Eastern image of the storm-god whose chariot is the clouds, here appropriated and monotheistically reapplied to the LORD who alone governs the heavens.
26.literary_structure: Verses 26-28 form a hymnic description of the incomparable God: incomparability declaration (v. 26a), attribute of transcendence (v. 26b — "who rides through the heavens"), attribute of immanence (v. 27a — "the eternal God is your dwelling place"), attribute of power (v. 27b — "underneath are the everlasting arms"), and resulting action (v. 27c — "he drives out the enemy"). Verse 28 describes Israel's resulting security (safe dwelling, grain and wine). Verse 29 closes the book's penultimate poem with a beatitude: "Happy are you, O Israel!" — matching the blessings of chapters 28 and 30 and forming the final positive note before the death of Moses in chapter 34.
26.theological_application: Gill's exposition of "everlasting arms" (v. 27) is theologically rich: "these are the arms of his power and grace, which are put under his people to support and uphold them under all their trials and difficulties." Matthew Henry applies the "dwelling place" image to the believer's security: "Those that make God their refuge, and put themselves under his protection, are safe. He is a God of all-sufficient power." Calvin finds in the phrase "there is none like God" the root of all genuine worship: comparison reveals incomparability, and incomparability demands exclusive devotion. The closing "happy are you" summarizes the whole benediction: covenant relationship with this God is the source of all true human happiness.
26.christology: "The eternal God is your dwelling place, and underneath are the everlasting arms" (v. 27) finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, in whom "the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily" (Col 2:9) and who promises permanent dwelling for his people: "I go to prepare a place for you... that where I am you may be also" (John 14:2-3). The "everlasting arms" upholding Israel in the wilderness become in the New Testament the hands of the Shepherd who holds his sheep: "I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand" (John 10:28). The final beatitude "happy are you, O Israel!" anticipates the Beatitudes of Matthew 5:3-12, where covenant happiness is redefined around the character of the kingdom's King.
